explanation: The requirement states that before the controller monitors for a sudden drop in pressure above ITS (monitorInhaleTrigger), it must wait for a duration: the trigger window (0.7 sec). In LTL, this is best captured as: whenever the monitorInhaleTrigger condition becomes true (i.e., the controller starts monitoring), it must be the case that in the previous 0.7 seconds, the controller has not been monitoring (i.e., !monitorInhaleTrigger holds for 0.7 sec), and then after that, monitoring can start. Template 3 expresses precisely this temporal waiting window before a condition occurs: it means either the property never happens globally, or (more commonly) if the property holds, then it has been preceded by a waiting window of at least N_DURATION steps of its negation. In this context, bool_exp3 is 'monitorInhaleTrigger' and N_DURATION is 7 (assuming each step is 0.1 seconds, which gives 0.7 seconds total).

The requirement states that the controller must wait 0.7 seconds (the trigger window) before starting to monitor for a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (ITS). The atomic proposition 'monitorInhaleTrigger' matches the start of monitoring for this drop. Thus, we require that 'monitorInhaleTrigger' cannot be true until at least 0.7 seconds have passed. The best matching LTL template is the one that guarantees for a duration (N_DURATION) that a state does not occur, then enforces that it eventually occurs. We use template 3 with bool_exp3 = 'monitorInhaleTrigger' and set N_DURATION to 7 (if we assume each step is 0.1s; this can be tuned according to actual system step size). This ensures that monitoring does not start until after the trigger window has passed, matching the language in the requirement ('Before monitoring... wait 0.7 sec').

The requirement states that the controller must wait for a 'trigger window' of 0.7 seconds before it begins monitoring for a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (ITS). Among the atomic propositions, 'monitorInhaleTrigger' represents the monitoring for that drop, so the LTL property should express that prior to 'monitorInhaleTrigger' becoming true, a delay of at least 0.7 seconds (the trigger window) is enforced. In LTL, this is best translated using a response pattern: whenever 'monitorInhaleTrigger' becomes true, it must be the case that for the previous 0.7 seconds (N_DURATION=7 if 0.1 sec LTL steps), the controller has been waiting. Since 'waiting' is not directly represented, this property ensures that 'monitorInhaleTrigger' can only occur after at least 0.7 seconds have passed since the last event that may disable monitoring. This fits the standard LTL pattern: "Globally, it is always the case that if 'monitorInhaleTrigger' happens, then 'monitorInhaleTrigger' was not true in the last 0.7 seconds, and within 0.7 seconds, it becomes true". Thus, template 3 is the best fit, where bool_exp3 is 'monitorInhaleTrigger' and N_DURATION=7 (for 0.1 s resolution, adjust as needed).

The requirement mandates that monitoring for a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (monitorInhaleTrigger) does not occur until after a trigger window of 0.7 seconds (the specified wait) has passed. To capture this in LTL using the provided atomic propositions, we interpret 'wait for the trigger window' as specifying that, regardless of any other state, 'monitorInhaleTrigger' cannot be true until at least 0.7 seconds (which we fix as N_DURATION=7 for 0.1s time steps as an example; can be adjusted based on implementation but must be >0) have elapsed. 

Template 3 precisely expresses this constraint: 'monitorInhaleTrigger' is not allowed for the first N_DURATION (here, 7) time units, but must become possible/allowed after that window. Thus, 'Globally for 0 to N_DURATION, NOT monitorInhaleTrigger; eventually (within N_DURATION+1), monitorInhaleTrigger' matches the natural language requirement that monitoring is not started before the end of the window.

The requirement states that 'before monitoring a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity ITS, the controller shall wait for the trigger window (0.7 sec)'. The atomic proposition 'monitorInhaleTrigger' represents the start of monitoring for the drop. We capture that, in any execution, whenever 'monitorInhaleTrigger' becomes true, it is only permitted after a 0.7-second delay. In LTL, we can encode that globally, 'monitorInhaleTrigger' implies that for the previous 7 steps (assuming each step is 0.1s), 'monitorInhaleTrigger' was not true (i.e., the system waited 0.7 seconds before becoming true). This can be expressed using the template: '((G[0,N_DURATION] (! bool_exp3)) | (G (! bool_exp3))) & (F[0,N_DURATION+1] bool_exp3)', which ensures 'bool_exp3' (here, 'monitorInhaleTrigger') is only allowed after a waiting period N_DURATION (with N_DURATION=7 for 0.7 sec).

The natural language requirement states that before the controller starts monitoring for a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (represented by 'monitorInhaleTrigger'), it must wait for a duration termed the 'trigger window', which is exactly 0.7 seconds. In LTL, 'before' with a specific wait translates to requiring that the property 'monitorInhaleTrigger' must not happen for a fixed time window (here, 0.7 sec, we set N_DURATION = 7 if we assume a time-step is 0.1 sec), and only after that can 'monitorInhaleTrigger' be true. The best-fitting template for this behavior is template 3:   (((G[0,N_DURATION] (! bool_exp3)) | (G (! bool_exp3))) & (F[0,N_DURATION+1] bool_exp3)),   where bool_exp3 = 'monitorInhaleTrigger', ensuring that 'monitorInhaleTrigger' does not occur in the first N_DURATION steps (the trigger window), and then within N_DURATION+1 it does occur, modeling the 'wait before monitoring'.

The requirement specifies that before the controller monitors a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (represented by monitorInhaleTrigger), it must wait for a trigger window of 0.7 seconds. This can be formalized such that the monitoring (monitorInhaleTrigger) is enabled only after a duration of 0.7 seconds, which is represented by using a bounded 'Globally' operator to block monitorInhaleTrigger for 0.7 seconds (let's assume N_DURATION = 7 if sampling is 100ms per tick, i.e., 7 * 0.1s = 0.7s), and then requiring that eventually (within the next time point) monitorInhaleTrigger becomes active (i.e., the behavior is not permanently blocked). This ensures that monitoring only starts after the trigger window. We use template 3 to express this behavior, blocking monitorInhaleTrigger globally for the window duration, but requiring it to happen right after, matching the 'before you do X, wait Y time' pattern.

The requirement states that 'before monitoring a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity ITS, the controller shall wait for the trigger window (0.7 sec)'. This indicates that whenever the system begins monitoring for the inhale trigger (monitorInhaleTrigger), there must have been a delay (wait) — specifically, the action should only occur after a 0.7 second window. The atomic proposition representing the monitoring is 'monitorInhaleTrigger'. The LTL template most suitable for this precondition logic is:

(G (((! bool_exp1) & (X bool_exp1)) -> (X (F[0,N_DURATION] bool_exp3))) & (bool_exp1 -> (F[0,N_DURATION] bool_exp3)))

But since the requirement is that monitorInhaleTrigger is not allowed before the 0.7s window, a more canonical form is to say: 'Globally, if monitorInhaleTrigger starts, it must be after a delay at least N_DURATION.' This can be encoded that whenever monitorInhaleTrigger becomes true, it must have been false for at least 0.7 sec prior, but without explicit windowing, the best matching template is: 'It is always not the case that monitorInhaleTrigger holds for the first 0.7 seconds' (i.e., G[0,N_DURATION] (! monitorInhaleTrigger)), and then eventually it may occur.
Therefore, the best-fitting template is:
3	'(((G[0,N_DURATION] (! bool_exp3)) | (G (! bool_exp3))) & (F[0,N_DURATION+1] bool_exp3))' with bool_exp3 = monitorInhaleTrigger and N_DURATION = 7 (assuming a time unit of 0.1s per tick, which is a typical discretization, so 0.7s is 7 steps).
This formula says, 'For the first 0.7s, monitoring the inhale trigger does not start, and within the next tick it may happen.'

The natural language requirement specifies that the controller must observe a waiting period ('trigger window', 0.7 seconds) before it begins monitoring (by setting 'monitorInhaleTrigger') for a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (ITS). To represent 'before monitoring, wait for 0.7s', we use a bounded 'globally' operator to ensure 'monitorInhaleTrigger' does not become true during the first 0.7s, and then is allowed to become true after. In LTL, this corresponds to holding '!monitorInhaleTrigger' globally (G) for the duration of the trigger window, and optionally allowing monitoring to begin afterward. This is best captured with template 3: '(((G[0,N_DURATION] (!bool_exp3)) | (G (!bool_exp3))) & (F[0,N_DURATION+1] bool_exp3))', where bool_exp3 is 'monitorInhaleTrigger'. We set N_DURATION=7, representing 0.7 sec if the unit is deciseconds (adapt this value as appropriate for system granularity).

The requirement states that, before monitoring for a sudden drop in pressure above the inhale trigger sensitivity (as indicated by 'monitorInhaleTrigger'), the controller must wait for a trigger window of 0.7 seconds. This can be formalized as: whenever 'monitorInhaleTrigger' becomes true, it must be preceded by a continuous interval of at least 0.7 seconds (N_DURATION=7 steps for 100ms steps, for example) in which monitoring was not active, i.e., (!monitorInhaleTrigger) held for N_DURATION steps before 'monitorInhaleTrigger' becomes true. This is well-captured by template 3, which captures a waiting period before transitioning to monitoring: it asserts that within every window of N_DURATION+1 steps, 'monitorInhaleTrigger' can only occur if there was a stretch of N_DURATION steps prior without it.
